examples.title: Ricart-Agrawala Algorithm Pseudocode
examples.description: Pseudocode for process requesting critical section entry in Ricart-Agawala algorithm
examples.code:
PROCEDURE RequestCS():
 state := WANTED
 timestamp := logical_clock + 1
 SEND REQUEST(ts, my_id) TO ALL OTHER PROCESSES
 WAIT UNTIL received REPLY from all processes
 state := HELD
 ENTER CRITICAL SECTION

PROCEDURE OnReceiveREQUEST(ts, sender_id):
 IF state = HELD OR (state = WANTED AND (ts, my_id) < (timestamp, sender_id)):
 ADD (ts, sender_id) TO DEFERRAL_QUEUE
 ELSE:
 SEND REPLY TO sender_id

PROCEDURE ReleaseCS():
 EXIT CRITICAL SECTION
 state := RELEASED
 FOR EACH (ts, pid) IN DEFERRAL_QUEUE IN ORDER:
 SEND REPLY TO pid
 CLEAR DEFERRAL_QUEUE
examples.explanation: Shows how a process requests CS by multicasting REQUEST to all, waits for all REPLYs, enters CS, and defers replies to higher priority requests while in CS

examples.title: Lamport Logical Clock Update Rules
examples.description: Implementation of Lamport's logical clock rules for event ordering
examples.code:
// Initialize
logical_clock := 0

// Event: Internal event or sending message
PROCEDURE Event():
 logical_clock := logical_clock + 1
 RETURN logical_clock

// Event: Send message
PROCEDURE SendMessage(msg):
 logical_clock := logical_clock + 1
 msg.timestamp := logical_clock
 SEND msg TO destination
 RETURN msg

// Event: Receive message
PROCEDURE ReceiveMessage(msg):
 logical_clock := max(logical_clock, msg.timestamp) + 1
 RETURN logical_clock
examples.explanation: Demonstrates the two fundamental rules: increment before sending/receiving, and take max on receiving

examples.title: Token Ring Mutual Exclusion
examples.description: Pseudocode for token-based mutual exclusion in a logical ring
examples.code:
// Process states
STATE := {IDLE, WAITING, HELD}
token_present := FALSE

// Token handling
PROCEDURE OnReceiveToken():
 token_present := TRUE
 IF STATE = WAITING THEN
 ENTER CRITICAL SECTION
 STATE := HELD
 ELSE
 // Pass token to next process
 token_present := FALSE
 SEND TOKEN TO next_process_in_ring

PROCEDURE RequestCS():
 STATE := WAITING
 IF NOT token_present THEN
 // Wait for token
 WHILE NOT token_present:
 WAIT

PROCEDURE ExitCS():
 STATE := IDLE
 token_present := FALSE
 SEND TOKEN TO next_process_in_ring
examples.explanation: Shows how token circulates in ring, and only token holder can enter CS. After exiting, token is passed to next process.
examples.timeComplexity: O(N) worst case (token may be far)

examples.title: Maekawa Quorum-Based Algorithm
examples.description: Pseudocode showing quorum-based request and voting in Maekawa algorithm
examples.code:
// Each process has a request set R[i] (quorum)
// Any two quorums must intersect

PROCEDURE RequestCS():
 state := WANTED
 timestamp := logical_clock + 1
 SEND REQUEST(ts, my_id) TO ALL processes IN R[my_id]
 SET reply_count := 0
 WHILE reply_count < |R[my_id]|:
 WAIT
 ENTER CRITICAL SECTION

PROCEDURE OnReceiveREQUEST(ts, sender_id):
 IF state = HELD OR have_voted THEN
 ADD (ts, sender_id) TO pending_queue
 ELSE:
 SEND REPLY TO sender_id
 have_voted := TRUE

PROCEDURE OnReceiveREPLY():
 reply_count := reply_count + 1

PROCEDURE ReleaseCS():
 state := RELEASED
 FOR EACH (ts, pid) IN pending_queue IN TIMESTAMP ORDER:
 SEND REPLY TO pid
 have_voted := FALSE
 CLEAR pending_queue
examples.explanation: Uses quorum instead of all processes - reduces messages from N-1 to quorum size (~√N). Voting ensures mutual exclusion through quorum intersection.
